summary: I served as the district secretary of the International Labor Defense, or ILD, which was an organization providing legal, bail, and prison relief for those arrested during labor struggles in the early 1930s. The ILD was deeply involved in supporting union organizing and strikes, including the waterfront strikes in San Francisco. Through the ILD, I became an honorary member of the International Longshoremen's Association San Francisco Strike Committee, working closely on defense issues related to arrests. The ILD operated in both Los Angeles and San Francisco, facing challenges from police Red Squads in both cities.

I was connected to the International Longshoremen's Association through my role with the ILD, becoming an honorary member of the San Francisco Strike Committee during the 1934 waterfront strike. The ILA was central to the labor struggles on the waterfront, and I worked with their subcommittee on defense issues concerning arrests. The strike was a significant event, and I helped publicize efforts to support the strike and discourage scabbing, especially in communities like Japantown and Chinatown. The ILA's struggles were intertwined with broader political and labor movements of the time.

I moved to San Francisco in 1933 from Los Angeles and became deeply involved in the labor and political movements there. San Francisco was a hub of waterfront activity, union organizing, and political agitation, especially around the 1934 strike. I worked with various organizations and committees in the city, including the Communist Party and the ILD, and witnessed firsthand the tensions in areas like Japantown and Chinatown. The city also hosted important venues like California Hall and Scottish Hall, which were centers for political meetings and rallies.

Before moving to San Francisco in 1933, I was active in Los Angeles as the district secretary of the International Labor Defense. The labor and political climate in L.A. was challenging, with raids and Red Squad activity similar to what I later encountered in San Francisco. Because of the distinct conditions in both cities, the ILD operated separate districts in L.A. and San Francisco. My experiences in Los Angeles prepared me for the struggles I would face on the waterfront and in union organizing in San Francisco.

Starting in 1933, I was the editor of Rodo Shimbun, a Japanese-language labor newspaper affiliated with the Communist Party USA. Editing Rodo Shimbun kept me busy during the critical years leading up to and including the 1934 waterfront strike. Through the paper, I helped publicize labor issues and strike activities, including distributing leaflets in Japanese and Chinese to discourage scabbing in Japantown and Chinatown. The publication was an important tool for communicating with Japanese-speaking workers and supporting union efforts.

From 1933 onward, I was involved with the Communist Party USA, particularly through its District 13 in California. The Party played a significant role in organizing labor, publishing materials like Rodo Shimbun, and supporting strikes such as the 1934 waterfront strike. We held weekly meetings and coordinated with union leaders and committees, including efforts to publicize the strike and combat scabbing. The Party also sponsored mass meetings at places like California Hall and Scottish Hall, and ran candidates for local offices like the Board of Supervisors.

I was a member of the District 13 Communist Party of California starting in 1933, which was a small group of five members meeting weekly. This district was active in labor organizing on the waterfront and played a key role in the 1934 strike. We coordinated closely with union leaders like John Schomaker and worked on publishing and defense committees to support arrested workers. Being part of District 13 gave me insight into the political strategies and grassroots organizing efforts in the San Francisco labor movement.

John Schomaker was the chairman of the publishing committee during the 1934 waterfront strike and a skilled writer. He was assigned to meet with the District 13 Communist Party bureau when a strike was imminent, which is how I learned about the waterfront situation. Schomaker approached me to help publicize the strike and the issue of Japanese seamen scabbing, asking me to produce leaflets in Japanese and Chinese. He later became an agent, and his leadership was crucial in the communication efforts supporting the strike.

Japantown in San Francisco was a significant community during the 1934 waterfront strike, where Japanese seamen were recruited as scabs. I was involved in producing leaflets in Japanese to distribute in Japantown urging support for the strike and discouraging scabbing. The neighborhood was an important site of labor and political activity, and the Japanese members of the Agriculture Workers Industrial Union were mostly Japan-born residents there. Japantown's role highlighted the ethnic and linguistic diversity involved in the labor struggles of the time.

Chinatown was another key area during the 1934 waterfront strike where scabbing was a concern, with efforts to distribute leaflets in Chinese to gain support for the strike. Alongside Japantown, Chinatown was targeted for outreach to prevent strikebreaking and to build solidarity among workers of different ethnic backgrounds. The strike organizers recognized the importance of involving Chinatown residents in the labor movement and used multilingual materials to communicate. This showed the broad coalition-building necessary for the strike's success.

Many Japanese members, mostly Japan-born, were part of the Agriculture Workers Industrial Union, which was affiliated with the Trade Union Unity League. This union was active during the early 1930s and connected to the broader labor movement, including waterfront organizing. Though primarily focused on agricultural workers, its membership overlapped with the Japanese community involved in the waterfront strike. The union was part of the Communist Party's efforts to organize workers across industries and ethnic lines.

The Trade Union Unity League was an umbrella organization that included the Agriculture Workers Industrial Union, which had many Japanese members. It represented a Communist Party effort to organize workers in various industries during the 1930s. The League supported militant unionism and was active in promoting labor solidarity during the period leading up to and including the 1934 waterfront strike. It helped coordinate efforts across different unions and ethnic groups on the West Coast.

The Marine Workers Industrial Union was more active on the East Coast in 1933 but became more noticeable on the West Coast during the 1934 waterfront strike. I don't recall attending their meetings before the strike, but they played a role in waterfront labor organizing. The union was part of the broader labor movement that sought to improve conditions for maritime workers. Their presence added to the complex network of unions involved in the waterfront struggles.

The Red Squad was a police unit that aggressively broke up street meetings and demonstrations in both Los Angeles and San Francisco during the early 1930s. They frequently arrested labor activists and union supporters on charges like disturbing the peace or refusing to move. Their actions violated the rights of workers to assemble and protest, which was a major concern for organizations like the ILD. The Red Squad's repression made organizing difficult but also galvanized resistance among labor activists.

The Scottsboro Boys case was a significant rallying point for labor and civil rights activists during the 1930s. We held mass rallies on the waterfront at noon hour to raise awareness and support for their cause. The case symbolized the broader struggle against injustice and was often linked with other labor and political campaigns like those for Tom Mooney and the Kentucky miners. It was part of the issues I spoke about when addressing workers and organizing public support.

Tom Mooney was a labor activist whose case inspired many rallies and conferences in the early 1930s. I attended a free Tom Mooney conference shortly after arriving in San Francisco in 1933, which introduced me to the local labor scene. His struggle was emblematic of the fight for justice for workers and political prisoners. Mooney's case was often mentioned alongside other causes like the Scottsboro Boys during mass meetings and protests on the waterfront.

I arrived in Oakland early one morning by train on my way to a Tom Mooney conference in San Francisco. When I got off the train, I witnessed the shape-up at the ferry building, which was a daily event where longshoremen were selected for work by ship owners. I initially thought it was a riot because of the shouting and tension, but later understood it was the harsh reality of waterfront labor conditions. Oakland was a gateway to the San Francisco waterfront and gave me my first impression of the struggles workers faced.

Mother Bloor was a visitor from the national Communist Party office who came out to the San Francisco waterfront to participate in street meetings. Her presence helped draw attention and support for labor causes during the early 1930s. I remember her coming to the progressive bookshop on the waterfront where workers gathered to listen to speeches and pick up materials. She was part of the broader Communist Party efforts to engage and organize waterfront workers.

Sam Darcy was a powerful orator and a Communist Party speaker who inspired large crowds at mass meetings in San Francisco before and during the 1934 strike. I remember his speeches at California Hall and Scottish Hall, where hundreds would raise their hands to join the Party. Darcy was not a rabble rouser but a compelling speaker who helped grow the Party's membership and support among workers. I still correspond with him, and he remains an important figure in the labor movement history here.

Nate Branch was one of the notable speakers on the San Francisco waterfront during the labor struggles of the 1930s. Alongside Jim Branch and Sam Darcy, he was known for his effective speaking style that helped rally workers. I remember attending meetings where he spoke, and his contributions were valuable to the movement. These men brought different styles but shared a commitment to advancing workers' rights and Communist Party goals.

Jim Branch was an Englishman and a skilled speaker involved in the labor movement on the San Francisco waterfront. His English accent was noticeable but not thick, and he spoke alongside figures like Sam Darcy and Nate Branch. I recall his participation in meetings and rallies that energized the workers during the early 1930s. Together, these leaders helped build support for union organizing and Communist Party activities.

California Hall in San Francisco was a key venue for mass meetings held by the Communist Party before the 1934 strike. We used to pack the hall for rallies where speakers like Sam Darcy would address hundreds of workers and supporters. The hall was near City Hall and served as an important gathering place for political and labor events. Interestingly, before the strike, the Nazi Party had its headquarters there, which added a tense political backdrop to our activities.

Before the 1934 waterfront strike, the Nazi Party had its headquarters at California Hall in San Francisco. This was a disturbing presence given the rise of fascism globally and the anti-fascist stance we took in our labor and political organizing. We were aware of the threat fascism posed and actively spoke against it in our rallies and street meetings. The Nazi Party's presence in the city underscored the urgency of our anti-war and anti-fascist campaigns during the early 1930s.

Scottish Hall was another important venue in San Francisco where the Communist Party held meetings and rallies during the early 1930s. We often gathered there to discuss labor issues, political strategies, and to build support for the 1934 waterfront strike. It was a central location for the Party's activities and helped foster a strong sense of community among activists. Along with California Hall, it was a hub for organizing and public speaking.

The Board of Supervisors in San Francisco was a political body for which Communist Party members, including myself later on, ran for office. During the 1930s, the Party supported candidates for the Board, and sometimes local unions like the longshoremen endorsed our slate. Although I ran as a non-partisan candidate, I was known as a Communist and had the backing of the labor movement. The Board represented an important arena for advancing workers' rights and political goals beyond the waterfront.
